section.title: Two Lines, Cain and Seth
section.sectionRationale: Two family lines are set side by side. Cain's line builds cities and culture and climbs to Lamech's boast of vengeance, while Seth's line ends with people calling on the name of the LORD. This closing section of chapter four traces where the two roads out of Eden are heading.
section.thesis: Away from God's presence one line builds a proud, violent civilization while another begins to call on the name of the LORD, and the whole Bible will follow these two roads to their ends.
section.frameNote: Two panels: the line of Cain from Enoch to Lamech's song of vengeance; and the line of Seth to Enosh, where worship of the LORD begins again.

verses.text: And Cain had relations with his wife, and she conceived and gave birth to Enoch. Then Cain built a {{city|city}} and named it after his son Enoch.
Now to Enoch was born Irad, and Irad was the father of Mehujael, and Mehujael was the father of Methusael, and Methusael was the father of Lamech.
And Lamech married two women, one named Adah and the other Zillah.
Adah gave birth to Jabal; he was the father of those who dwell in tents and raise livestock.
And his brother’s name was Jubal; he was the father of all who play the harp and flute.
And Zillah gave birth to Tubal-cain, a forger of every implement of bronze and iron. And the sister of Tubal-cain was Naamah.
Then Lamech said to his wives: “Adah and Zillah, hear my voice; wives of Lamech, listen to my speech. For I have slain a man for wounding me, a young man for striking me.
If Cain is {{avenged|avenged}} sevenfold, then Lamech seventy-sevenfold.”

units.label: The line of Seth
units.note: calling on the name of the LORD

verses.text: And Adam again had relations with his wife, and she gave birth to a son and named him Seth, saying, “God has granted me another {{seed|seed}} in place of Abel, since Cain killed him.”
And to Seth also a son was born, and he called him Enosh. At that time men began to {{call|call upon}} the name of the LORD.

city.gloss: A city or walled settlement. Cain, sentenced to be a restless wanderer, instead builds a city to secure himself and names it after his son. Human culture begins here, good gifts of art and craft mixed with a drive to build a home apart from God's presence.

avenged.gloss: To avenge, to take vengeance. God had promised sevenfold protection for Cain; Lamech seizes that language and boasts of seventy-sevenfold revenge for a mere wound. He turns a promise of restraint into a bragging charter for unlimited violence. Jesus overturns the number in Matthew 18:22.

seed.gloss: Offspring, seed. Eve confesses that God has granted another seed in place of murdered Abel. The promised line of 3:15 does not die with Abel; it continues through Seth, and Scripture will trace it down to Christ.

call.gloss: To call, to call out, to proclaim. In Seth's line people began to call on the name of the LORD, that is, to worship and pray to Him by name. Against Lamech's boast in his own name, a people rises who name and seek the LORD.

basic.context: The Bible now traces two families. Cain's descendants build cities, raise animals, make music, and forge tools, and they end with Lamech, a proud man who boasts about killing. Then we meet Seth's line, and the section ends with people beginning to worship God by name. The question is where each road is going.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real genealogy, but it is arranged to make a contrast. Cain's line shows culture racing ahead while violence deepens. Seth's line is quieter but turns toward God. The lesson is that progress in skill and comfort is not the same as being right with God.
basic.originalLanguages: Cain builds a city, an ir, to make himself safe. Lamech twists God's word about being avenged, naqam, into a boast of seventy-sevenfold revenge. But Eve calls Seth another seed, zera, in place of Abel, and in Seth's line people begin to call, qara, on the name of the LORD, to worship Him by name.
basic.typology: The seed given in place of Abel carries forward the promise of 3:15 that runs to Christ. And the two lines, one calling on the LORD and one boasting in itself, foreshadow the two humanities the whole Bible tracks, ending in the city of man and the city of God.
basic.christ: Lamech promises seventy-sevenfold vengeance; Jesus tells Peter to forgive seventy-seven times, undoing Lamech's boast with grace. And the seed granted in place of Abel keeps alive the line that leads to Jesus, the true seed who saves rather than avenges.
basic.god: God keeps His promise even after murder: the chosen line is not extinguished but continues through Seth. And God is the One whose name people begin to call upon, showing that He remains near to be worshipped even in a world drifting into violence.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 18 answers Lamech's seventy-sevenfold with forgiveness. Hebrews 11 lists the worshippers of faith who called on God. Jude warns against the way of Cain. Luke 3 traces Jesus' line back through Seth to Adam.
basic.oneStory: Two roads leave Eden. One builds a proud, self-secured, violent civilization; the other begins to call on the name of the LORD. The Bible will follow both to their ends, and the line of worship runs on toward Christ, while the line of boasting foreshadows the world He came to redeem.

context.literary: A deliberately balanced pair of genealogies closing chapter four. Cain's line is expanded with cultural achievements and a poem of vengeance; Seth's line is brief but ends on a note of worship, a quiet counterpoint to Lamech's loud boast.
context.historical: The list reflects the real beginnings of settled life, herding, music, and metalworking. For Israel it explained the origins of civilization while warning that technical progress can run alongside deep moral decline.
context.canonical: The first sketch of the two humanities that Scripture traces to the end, the line of the flesh boasting in itself and the line of faith calling on the LORD, later drawn out as the city of man and the city of God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To contrast two lines flowing out of Eden, showing that human culture advances while sin also advances, and that the promised seed and true worship survive through Seth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The genealogies describe what happened; only Lamech's speech is held up implicitly as a warning. The abiding call is not to boast like Lamech but to call on the name of the LORD like Seth's line.

debates.question: Is Cain's line condemned simply for its culture and cities?

views.label: Culture itself is under suspicion
views.case: The city is built by the restless wanderer in defiance of his sentence, and the line climaxes in Lamech's violence, suggesting the whole project is a bid for security apart from God.

views.label: Culture is a good gift, misdirected by sin
views.case: Herding, music, and metalwork are genuine gifts reflecting the image of God, and later Scripture affirms craft and art; the condemnation falls on Lamech's pride and violence, not on culture as such.

debates.bearing: Read either way, the passage warns that skill and progress are no substitute for a right heart; the same hands that make music and tools can also boast in bloodshed.

study.originalLanguages: The section is shaped by a few key words. Cain, doomed to wander, builds an ir, a city, grasping for the security God's sentence denied him. His descendant Lamech seizes the word naqam, vengeance, and inflates God's sevenfold protection of Cain into a boast of seventy-sevenfold revenge for a mere wound. Over against this, Eve names Seth another zera, seed, in place of Abel, and the promised line lives on. The chapter closes on qara, to call: people begin to call on the name of the LORD, a movement of worship set directly against Lamech's worship of himself.

typology.type: The seed granted in place of Abel
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true seed of the woman, carried forward through the line of Seth, Luke 3:38.

typology.type: The two lines, Cain and Seth
typology.fulfillment: The two humanities of Scripture, the world in Adam and the people of faith, gathered finally into the city of man and the city of God.

typology.type: Calling on the name of the LORD
typology.fulfillment: The worshipping people of God, fulfilled as everyone who calls on the name of the Lord is saved, Romans 10:13.

study.christ: Two lines from this passage point to Christ. First, Lamech's boast of seventy-sevenfold vengeance is deliberately overturned by Jesus, who tells Peter to forgive not seven times but seventy-seven times, Matthew 18:22; the Son turns Lamech's charter for revenge into a call to endless mercy, and secures that mercy at the cross. Second, the seed granted in place of Abel keeps alive the promised line of 3:15, the line that Luke traces through Seth all the way to Jesus, the true seed who does not avenge Himself on His enemies but dies for them. To call on the name of the LORD, begun here, finds its fullness in calling on the name of Jesus for salvation.
study.god: God is faithful to His promise. Though Abel is murdered and Cain is exiled, God grants another seed through Seth, and the chosen line does not fail. God is also the One near enough to be named and sought: while Cain's line builds a name for itself, Seth's line begins to call on the name of the LORD, and God receives their worship. He remains the God who keeps His covenant and welcomes those who seek Him, even in a darkening world.

crossReferences.note: Jesus answers Lamech's seventy-sevenfold vengeance with seventy-seven-fold forgiveness.
The genealogy of Jesus runs through Seth back to Adam and to God.
Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved, the fulfillment of calling on the LORD begun in Seth's line.
Woe to those who have gone the way of Cain, whose line the passage traces.

study.oneStory: Two roads leave Eden. Cain's line races ahead in culture and comfort but hardens into Lamech's proud violence, a picture of the city of man building a name for itself. Seth's line is quieter, but there people begin to call on the name of the LORD, and through that line the promised seed presses on toward Jesus, in whom vengeance gives way to mercy and all who call on His name are saved.

quiz.q: What is the main contrast between the line of Cain and the line of Seth?

options.text: Cain's line boasts in itself and its violence, while Seth's line begins to call on the name of the LORD.
options.note: The two lines picture two roads, self-exaltation and worship.

options.text: Cain's line was poor while Seth's line was wealthy.
options.note: The contrast is spiritual, not about wealth; Cain's line is actually rich in culture.

options.text: Cain's line had no children while Seth's line multiplied.
options.note: Both lines have descendants; the difference is their direction toward or away from God.

options.text: There is no real contrast; both lines are the same.
options.note: The passage is built to set the two lines against each other.

quiz.q: What does Lamech's song reveal about the line of Cain?

options.text: It has grown proud and boasts openly in vengeance and violence.
options.note: Lamech turns God's promise of protection into a charter for revenge.

options.text: It has become sorrowful and repentant over sin.
options.note: The opposite; Lamech boasts rather than repents.

options.text: It has simply grown skilled in music and metalwork.
options.note: The skills are real, but the song exposes the deeper problem of pride and violence.

options.text: It has turned back to worship the LORD.
options.note: That describes Seth's line, not Lamech.

quiz.q: Why does Eve call Seth another seed in place of Abel?

options.text: The promised line of the woman's seed continues through Seth after Abel's death.
options.note: God preserves the chosen line that leads toward Christ.

options.text: Seth was simply her favorite child.
options.note: There is hope in her words, but the point is God's faithfulness to the promise, not favoritism.

options.text: Seth would avenge Abel's murder on Cain.
options.note: Nothing here speaks of vengeance; Seth carries the line of promise.

options.text: It means nothing beyond naming a baby.
options.note: Eve's words point to God granting a seed to continue the promise.

quiz.q: How does Jesus respond to Lamech's cry of seventy-sevenfold vengeance?

options.text: He calls His people to forgive seventy-seven times, replacing vengeance with mercy.
options.note: Matthew 18:22 deliberately overturns Lamech's boast.

options.text: He commands even greater vengeance on enemies.
options.note: The reverse; Jesus overturns vengeance with forgiveness.

options.text: He says vengeance should be limited to seven times only.
options.note: Jesus does not cap revenge; He replaces it with abundant forgiveness.

options.text: He never addresses anything like Lamech's boast.
options.note: His seventy-seven times clearly answers Lamech's seventy-sevenfold.

quiz.q: What began in the line of Seth at the time of Enosh?

options.text: People began to call on the name of the LORD, that is, to worship Him by name.
options.note: Public worship of the LORD rises in Seth's line.

options.text: People began to build the first cities.
options.note: That was Cain's line; Seth's line is marked by worship.

options.text: People began to forge tools of bronze and iron.
options.note: That is Tubal-cain in Cain's line, not Seth's.

options.text: People stopped believing in God altogether.
options.note: The opposite; they began to call on the LORD.

questions: Cain's line was gifted with real skill in music, craft, and building, yet drifted from God. Where might your own talents and progress be quietly pulling you away from Him instead of toward Him?
Lamech boasted about getting his own back and multiplying revenge. Where are you keeping score against someone, and what would it look like to hand that to God instead?
The line of Seth is remembered for beginning to call on the name of the LORD. What does calling on God look like in the ordinary rhythm of your week?
God preserved the promised line even after murder and heartbreak. Where do you need to trust that God is still keeping His promises when your own story feels broken?
Two roads run through this passage, one building a name for itself and one seeking God's name. Which road are your daily choices actually laying down for those who come after you?

translationNotes.note: Began to call upon the name of the LORD can also be rendered began to proclaim the name of the LORD, or, in some traditions, to call themselves by the name of the LORD; each reading points to the beginning of public worship in Seth's line.
